topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard, glossy and smelling faintly of cheap ink, lay crumpled on my desk. Across the faded blue background, a picture of a sprawling, unfamiliar coastline dominated the frame. My stomach did a slow, heavy roll. Liam. Moving. Across the country. It wasn't a shock, exactly; we’d talked about big moves for ages, but seeing it materialized in paper made the reality of the separation press down on me like a physical weight. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over his contact. I didn't call immediately. Instead, I walked over to the window, staring out at the familiar cityscape, trying to anchor myself in the present moment before the impending distance could truly take hold. When I finally did dial, my voice came out softer than I intended, a little breathy. "Hey, man. So, you're actually doing it?" The silence on the other end stretched, thick with unspoken goodbyes. I just listened, letting the weight of the news settle, feeling a strange, protective warmth bloom in my chest despite the ache of impending loneliness.

[story 2]

I found the news tucked inside a library book—a sudden, stark declaration printed on a flyer. Sarah was leaving. Moving to Seattle. My hands started shaking slightly as I reread the details, the logistics of packing up a life and starting over in a place I’d never seen. A tightness constricted my throat, making it hard to draw a full breath. I spent the next hour just pacing my small apartment, running my hands over the worn spines of old novels, seeking some kind of comfort in the tangible things. When I finally called her, I kept my tone level, deliberately calm. "That’s… a big leap, Sarah. Are you sure about this?" I could hear the hesitation in her voice, the slight tremor that mirrored my own internal unrest. I didn't try to fill the gap with platitudes. I just offered space, a quiet acknowledgment of the magnitude of what she was facing, letting the shared gravity of the situation hang between us before she responded.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my screen, a simple text message confirming Liam’s departure date. For a long minute, I just stared at the screen, the blue light reflecting in my wide eyes. A profound sense of melancholy washed over me, a deep, quiet sorrow that settled in my bones. It felt like watching a favorite constellation slowly drift out of view. I didn't immediately reach for my laptop to look up travel routes or try to find a way to mitigate the distance. Instead, I went into the kitchen and started making his favorite coffee, the ritualistic movements of grinding beans and pouring water a strange form of grounding. When I finally typed out a reply, it was short, almost hesitant. "I'm going to miss having you around. Seriously." The slight vulnerability in my message felt exposed, but the relief that I had voiced the feeling was immediate, a small release of pressure that left me feeling strangely settled, if a little hollowed out.
